EDA - Infineon Process-Logic Track

Auto-generated by eda.py. Figures are in figures/.

1. Executive findings

This data is not a generic language-modelling problem. It is a small-vocabulary, grammar-heavy sequence problem with local determinism plus a handful of long-range structural choices.

  • The useful baseline is already strong: on a held-out 80/20 split, an order-3 family-conditioned n-gram reaches 79.5% top-1 and 98.4% top-5 next-step accuracy across all token positions.
  • Completion exact-match is a trap: at the official 60% and 80% cuts, held-out partial prefixes seen verbatim in train = 0. Exact route memorization is therefore not a viable completion strategy; evaluate process shape with edit distance and block-level accuracy.
  • The current 3,000 routes pass the official validator: no training sequence violates the 10 documented process rules. That makes the validator a clean anomaly oracle and a useful constrained-decoding filter.
  • Family conditioning should be explicit: 94 steps are shared by all families, but each family exposes a unique signal early (see section 4). A prepended family token is cheap and removes avoidable ambiguity before the route itself reveals the product family.
  • A small transformer is justified only if it beats this baseline where the baseline is weak: long-range optional choices, synonym selection, sequence completion coherence, and model-likelihood anomaly detection.

3. Predictability and baseline ceiling

Conditional entropy collapses quickly: average top-1 oracle accuracy rises from 69% at order 1 to 81% at order 3; order-2 top-5 is already 99.7%.

Local solution artifact check (synthetic eval set mirroring organiser protocol):

  • n-gram baseline: next-step top-1 73.2%, top-5 100.0%, completion exact-match 0.7%, block-accuracy 94.7%, anomaly F1 86.2%.
  • small transformer: next-step top-1 74.2%, top-5 100.0%, completion exact-match 0.2%, block-accuracy 94.2%, anomaly F1 93.7%.
  • validator oracle anomaly ceiling: F1 100.0%, rule-attribution accuracy 97.7%.

4. Completion risk and family signal

The organiser cuts are hard because the remaining suffix is still long, while exact prefix replay is unavailable on a held-out split.

familymedianfirstunique_stepp90firstunique_stepcoverage
MOSFET1112100%
IGBT1213100%
IC46100%

Interpretation: a model can infer the family from the route fairly early, but an explicit family token is still the cleaner setup for the first shared logistics and cleaning steps, and it gives a clear fallback path for the hidden OOD family.

5. Variation axes that create real ambiguity

These are the knobs that make local contexts ambiguous. They are also the best places to look for model-vs-baseline differences.

6. Modeling recommendation

1. Ship an order-3 n-gram baseline first. It is transparent, very hard to beat on top-5 next-step prediction, and establishes the "before" benchmark.

2. Train a step-token causal transformer with a family token. Use a tiny/small model first; the vocabulary is tiny, so scale experiments should focus on whether extra capacity improves completion coherence and anomaly likelihood, not just next-step top-5.

3. Use the official validator in two places: as an anomaly oracle/rule-attributor, and as a constrained-decoding or reranking filter for completions.

4. Report raw and semantic metrics side by side. Exact-match and token accuracy punish valid synonym/optional-step divergences; edit distance and block-level accuracy better reflect whether the process route is plausible.

5. Stress-test the variation axes. Hold out routes with POST EXPOSE BAKE, HARD BAKE, PRE ANNEAL CHECK, and via-fill synonym variants to show whether the model learned process logic rather than average local successors.
